title: K-sorted array
description: Sort nearly sorted array using heap

examples.explanation:
Step 1: Input array is [6, 5, 3, 2, 8, 10, 9].
Step 2: Apply quicksort (pivot first element 6): Partition into [5, 3, 2] (less than 6) and [8, 10, 9] (greater than 6).
Step 3: Recursively sort left subarray [5, 3, 2]: pivot 5 → [3, 2] and [] → sort [3, 2] → pivot 3 → [2] and [] → combine to [2, 3, 5].
Step 4: Recursively sort right subarray [8, 10, 9]: pivot 8 → [] and [10, 9] → sort [10, 9] → pivot 10 → [9] and [] → combine to [9, 10] → [8, 9, 10].
Step 5: Combine sorted subarrays: [2, 3, 5] + [6] + [8, 9, 10] = [2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Sort the entire array using a standard sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort, ignoring the nearly sorted property. Use this as a baseline when the k-sorted constraint is unknown or not leveraged.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) for in-place sorts like quicksort, or O(n) for mergesort

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort K-Sorted Array Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Sort a nearly sorted array where each element is at most k positions away from its sorted position using a standard sorting algorithm.
ladders.explanation:
The brute force approach to sorting a k-sorted array ignores the nearly sorted property and treats it as a regular unsorted array. The intuition is that any sorting algorithm can sort any array, regardless of its initial order. While this doesn't leverage the k-sorted property for efficiency, it provides a correct baseline solution. The algorithm involves applying a standard O(n log n) sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort to the entire array. For quicksort, we choose a pivot, partition the array into elements less than and greater than the pivot, then recursively sort the subarrays. Mergesort divides the array into halves, recursively sorts them, then merges the sorted halves. Time complexity is O(n log n) for both average cases, with quicksort having O(n²) worst-case but O(1) space (in-place), while mergesort has O(n) space. This connects to the problem by providing a working solution when k is unknown or the nearly sorted property isn't leveraged. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like empty arrays or single elements, and choosing unstable sorts when stability matters. Real code pattern uses Python's built-in sort or a custom implementation.

Pseudocode:
function bruteForceSort(arr):
    if length(arr) ≤ 1:
        return arr
    # Use built-in sort or implement quicksort/mergesort
    sort(arr)  # O(n log n) operation
    return arr

examples.trace:
Step 1: Input array is [6, 5, 3, 2, 8, 10, 9].
Step 2: Apply quicksort (pivot first element 6): Partition into [5, 3, 2] (less than 6) and [8, 10, 9] (greater than 6).
Step 3: Recursively sort left subarray [5, 3, 2]: pivot 5 → [3, 2] and [] → sort [3, 2] → pivot 3 → [2] and [] → combine to [2, 3, 5].
Step 4: Recursively sort right subarray [8, 10, 9]: pivot 8 → [] and [10, 9] → sort [10, 9] → pivot 10 → [9] and [] → combine to [9, 10] → [8, 9, 10].
Step 5: Combine sorted subarrays: [2, 3, 5] + [6] + [8, 9, 10] = [2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Implement Quicksort Partitioning
ladders.desc: Partition an array around a pivot element for the quicksort algorithm.
ladders.explanation:
Partitioning is the core operation in quicksort that rearranges an array so all elements less than a pivot come before it, and all greater come after. The intuition is to select a pivot (e.g., first element), then iterate through the array, swapping elements to maintain two regions: less-than-pivot and greater-than-pivot. This works because it systematically groups elements relative to the pivot, enabling recursive sorting. The algorithm: choose pivot (e.g., arr[low]), initialize partition index pi = low, iterate i from low+1 to high, if arr[i] < pivot, swap arr[i] with arr[pi+1] and increment pi, finally swap pivot with arr[pi] to place pivot in correct position. Time complexity is O(n) for one partition, space O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the recursive step in quicksort. Common mistakes include not handling equal elements correctly (can go to either side) and off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern uses in-place swaps.

Pseudocode:
function partition(arr, low, high):
    pivot = arr[low]
    pi = low
    for i from low+1 to high:
        if arr[i] < pivot:
            pi = pi + 1
            swap arr[pi] and arr[i]
    swap arr[low] and arr[pi]
    return pi

examples.trace:
Step 1: Input arr = [5, 3, 8, 4, 2], pivot = 5, pi = 0.
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=3 < 5 → pi=1, swap arr[1] and arr[1] (no change) → arr = [5, 3, 8, 4, 2].
Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=8 ≥ 5 → no change.
Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=4 < 5 → pi=2, swap arr[3] and arr[2] → arr = [5, 3, 4, 8, 2].
Step 5: i=4, arr[4]=2 < 5 → pi=3, swap arr[4] and arr[3] → arr = [5, 3, 4, 2, 8].
Step 6: Swap pivot arr[0] with arr[pi=3] → swap 5 and 2 → arr = [2, 3, 4, 5, 8], return pi=3.

Step 1: Input arr = [10, 7, 8, 9, 1, 5], pivot = 10, pi = 0.
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=7 < 10 → pi=1, swap arr[1] and arr[1] → arr = [10, 7, 8, 9, 1, 5].
Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=8 < 10 → pi=2, swap arr[2] and arr[2] → arr unchanged.
Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=9 < 10 → pi=3, swap arr[3] and arr[3] → unchanged.
Step 5: i=4, arr[4]=1 < 10 → pi=4, swap arr[4] and arr[4] → unchanged.
Step 6: i=5, arr[5]=5 < 10 → pi=5, swap arr[5] and arr[5] → unchanged.
Step 7: Swap pivot arr[0] with arr[pi=5] → swap 10 and 5 → arr = [5, 7, 8, 9, 1, 10], return pi=5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divide and Conquer Sorting Strategy
ladders.desc: Apply divide and conquer to sort an array by recursively breaking it into smaller subproblems.
ladders.explanation:
Divide and conquer is a paradigm where a problem is divided into smaller subproblems, solved recursively, and combined. For sorting, the intuition is that sorting smaller arrays is easier, and merging sorted arrays is efficient. This works because breaking the problem reduces complexity, and the combine step ensures correctness. The algorithm: if array size ≤ 1, return (base case); otherwise, divide array into two halves (e.g., mid = n/2), recursively sort each half, then merge the sorted halves. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to log n levels of recursion and O(n) work per level; space is O(n) for mergesort or O(log n) for quicksort's recursion stack. This connects to L1 as partitioning implements the divide step in quicksort. Common mistakes include not having a base case (causing infinite recursion) and incorrect division indices. Real code pattern uses recursion.

Pseudocode:
function divideAndConquerSort(arr, low, high):
    if low < high:
        mid = (low + high) // 2
        divideAndConquerSort(arr, low, mid)
        divideAndConquerSort(arr, mid+1, high)
        merge(arr, low, mid, high)  # or partition for quicksort

examples.trace:
Step 1: Input arr = [3, 1, 4, 2], low=0, high=3, low<high.
Step 2: mid = (0+3)//2 = 1.
Step 3: Recursively sort left half [3,1]: low=0, high=1, mid=0 → sort [3] (base case) and [1] (base case) → merge to [1,3].
Step 4: Recursively sort right half [4,2]: low=2, high=3, mid=2 → sort [4] and [2] → merge to [2,4].
Step 5: Merge halves [1,3] and [2,4]: compare 1<2 → [1], 3>2 → [1,2], 3<4 → [1,2,3], add 4 → [1,2,3,4].

ladders.title: Building Block — Compare and Swap Elements
ladders.desc: Compare two elements and swap them if they are out of order.
ladders.explanation:
Comparing and swapping is a fundamental operation in sorting algorithms to reorder elements. The intuition is that by repeatedly comparing adjacent or selected elements and swapping when necessary, we can gradually move elements to their correct positions. This works because it directly manipulates the array based on relative order. The algorithm: given two indices i and j in an array, compare arr[i] and arr[j]; if arr[i] > arr[j] (for ascending order), swap their values using a temporary variable. Time complexity is O(1) per operation, space O(1). This connects to L2 as it's used in merging or partitioning steps of divide and conquer sorts. Common mistakes include swapping without a temp variable (can lose data) and incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern uses a simple if-statement and swap.

Pseudocode:
function compareAndSwap(arr, i, j):
    if arr[i] > arr[j]:
        temp = arr[i]
        arr[i] = arr[j]
        arr[j] = temp

examples.trace:
Step 1: Input arr = [5, 3], i=0, j=1.
Step 2: Compare arr[0]=5 and arr[1]=3: 5 > 3, so swap.
Step 3: temp = arr[0] = 5.
Step 4: arr[0] = arr[1] = 3.
Step 5: arr[1] = temp = 5.
Step 6: Result arr = [3, 5].

Step 1: Input arr = [2, 4, 1, 3], i=1, j=2.
Step 2: Compare arr[1]=4 and arr[2]=1: 4 > 1, so swap.
Step 3: temp = arr[1] = 4.
Step 4: arr[1] = arr[2] = 1.
Step 5: arr[2] = temp = 4.
Step 6: Result arr = [2, 1, 4, 3].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access and Modify Array Elements
ladders.desc: Read from and write to specific positions in an array.
ladders.explanation:
Accessing and modifying array elements is a fundamental operation in programming, essential for sorting algorithms. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, allowing direct access via indices. This works because each index corresponds to a memory offset, enabling O(1) time operations. The algorithm: to read, use arr[index]; to write, assign arr[index] = value. Time complexity is O(1), space O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used in compare-and-swap operations. Common mistakes include accessing out-of-bounds indices (causing errors) and confusing zero-based indexing. Real code pattern uses square brackets for indexing.

Pseudocode:
# Read element at index i
value = arr[i]
# Write element at index i
arr[i] = newValue

examples.input: arr = [10, 20, 30], read index = 1, write index = 2 with value = 40
examples.trace:
Step 1: Input arr = [10, 20, 30].
Step 2: Read arr[1] = 20.
Step 3: Write arr[2] = 40.
Step 4: Result arr = [10, 20, 40].

examples.input: arr = [5, 3, 7, 1], read index = 0, write index = 3 with value = 9
examples.trace:
Step 1: Input arr = [5, 3, 7, 1].
Step 2: Read arr[0] = 5.
Step 3: Write arr[3] = 9.
Step 4: Result arr = [5, 3, 7, 9].

testCases.input: arr = [], read index = 0, write index = 0 with value = 1
arr = [2], read index = 0, write index = 0 with value = 5
arr = [1, 2], read index = 1, write index = 0 with value = 3
arr = [4, 5, 6], read index = 2, write index = 1 with value = 0
arr = [7, 8, 9, 10], read index = 3, write index = 2 with value = 11
arr = [0, 0, 0], read index = 0, write index = 2 with value = 1
arr = [3, 1, 4], read index = 1, write index = 1 with value = 2
arr = [9, 7, 5, 3], read index = 0, write index = 3 with value = 1
arr = [6, 2, 8, 4, 0], read index = 4, write index = 0 with value = 10
arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], read index = 2, write index = 4 with value = 6

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to use indices to access elements.
ladders.explanation:
An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index. The intuition is that it's like a row of boxes numbered starting from 0, where you can quickly find any box by its number. This works because the memory address of an element can be calculated as base address + index * element size. Key concepts: arrays have fixed size (in many languages), indices are zero-based (first element at index 0), and elements can be of any data type (e.g., integers). Time to access is O(1), space is O(n). This connects to L4 as it's the basis for access and modify operations. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing or accessing beyond array bounds. Real code pattern involves declaring arrays and using indices.

Pseudocode:
# Declare an array
arr = [3, 1, 4, 2]
# Access element at index 2
value = arr[2]  # returns 4
# Array length
n = length(arr)  # returns 4

examples.trace:
Step 1: Array arr has elements: index 0 = 5, index 1 = 10, index 2 = 15, index 3 = 20.
Step 2: Access arr[2] returns the element at index 2, which is 15.

Step 1: Array arr: index 0 = 2, index 1 = 4, index 2 = 6, index 3 = 8, index 4 = 10.
Step 2: Access arr[0] returns 2.
Step 3: Access arr[4] returns 10.

approaches.name: Optimized Insertion Sort O(nk)
approaches.summary: Leverage the nearly sorted property by using insertion sort, where each element is at most k positions away from its sorted position, reducing comparisons. This is efficient for small k values but degrades as k increases.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort K-Sorted Array Using Optimized Insertion Sort
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of insertion sort optimized for nearly sorted arrays where each element is at most k positions from its sorted position.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach leverages the nearly sorted property of the array. In a k-sorted array, each element is at most k positions away from where it would be in the fully sorted array. This means when we process an element at position i, we only need to compare it with elements in the window [i-k, i-1] rather than all previous elements. The algorithm works by iterating through the array from index 1 to n-1. For each element arr[i], we store it in a temporary variable key. Then we compare key with elements from i-1 down to max(0, i-k), shifting elements right if they're greater than key. Finally, we place key in its correct position. This reduces comparisons from O(n²) in standard insertion sort to O(nk) when k is small. The space complexity remains O(1) since we only use a constant amount of extra space. Connection to L1: This full solution calls the insertion subroutine for each element. Common mistakes include not limiting the comparison window to k elements, which defeats the optimization, or incorrectly handling edge cases when i < k. The algorithm is particularly efficient when k is small (k << n), making it practical for nearly sorted data like streaming data or partially sorted outputs from other algorithms.

examples.trace:
Start: [6,5,3,2,8,10,9], k=3
i=1: key=5, compare with arr[0]=6 (6>5) → shift 6 right → [6,6,3,2,8,10,9], insert 5 at index 0 → [5,6,3,2,8,10,9]
i=2: key=3, compare with arr[1]=6 (6>3) → shift 6 right → [5,6,6,2,8,10,9], compare with arr[0]=5 (5>3) → shift 5 right → [5,5,6,2,8,10,9], insert 3 at index 0 → [3,5,6,2,8,10,9]
i=3: key=2, compare with arr[2]=6 (6>2) → shift 6 right → [3,5,6,6,8,10,9], compare with arr[1]=5 (5>2) → shift 5 right → [3,5,5,6,8,10,9], compare with arr[0]=3 (3>2) → shift 3 right → [3,3,5,6,8,10,9], insert 2 at index 0 → [2,3,5,6,8,10,9]
i=4: key=8, compare with arr[3]=6 (6<8) → no shift, insert 8 at index 4 → [2,3,5,6,8,10,9]
i=5: key=10, compare with arr[4]=8 (8<10) → no shift, insert 10 at index 5 → [2,3,5,6,8,10,9]
i=6: key=9, compare with arr[5]=10 (10>9) → shift 10 right → [2,3,5,6,8,10,10], compare with arr[4]=8 (8<9) → no shift, insert 9 at index 5 → [2,3,5,6,8,9,10]
Final: [2,3,5,6,8,9,10]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert Element into Nearly Sorted Prefix
ladders.desc: Insert a single element into its correct position within the previous k elements of a partially sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the optimized insertion sort algorithm. The intuition is that in a k-sorted array, when processing element at index i, we only need to find its correct position within the window of the previous k elements (from i-1 down to i-k), rather than comparing with all previous elements. The algorithm takes the current element arr[i] as key, then iterates backwards from j = i-1 down to max(0, i-k). At each step, if arr[j] > key, we shift arr[j] one position to the right. Once we find a position where arr[j] <= key or we reach the beginning of the window, we insert key at position j+1. Time complexity is O(k) for each insertion since we compare at most k elements. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each element in the array in the full solution. Common mistakes include not limiting the backward search to k elements (which would make it O(i) instead of O(k)), or forgetting to handle the case when i < k where we should compare with all previous elements. The efficiency comes from exploiting the k-sorted property: we know key cannot belong more than k positions left, so we don't need to check further.

examples.trace:
Start: arr=[2,4,5,3,6], i=3 (element=3), k=2
key = arr[3] = 3
j starts at i-1 = 2
While j >= max(0, i-k) = max(0, 1) = 1 and arr[j] > key:
  j=2: arr[2]=5 > 3 → shift 5 right → arr[4]=6, arr[3]=5 → [2,4,5,5,6]
  j=1: arr[1]=4 > 3 → shift 4 right → arr[2]=5, arr[1]=4 → [2,4,4,5,6]
  j=0: j < 1, stop loop
Insert key at j+1 = 1 → arr[1]=3 → [2,3,4,5,6]
Result: [2,3,4,5,6]

Start: arr=[1,3,2,4,5], i=2 (element=2), k=1
key = arr[2] = 2
j starts at i-1 = 1
While j >= max(0, i-k) = max(0, 1) = 1 and arr[j] > key:
  j=1: arr[1]=3 > 2 → shift 3 right → arr[2]=3 → [1,3,3,4,5]
  j=0: j < 1, stop loop
Insert key at j+1 = 1 → arr[1]=2 → [1,2,3,4,5]
Result: [1,2,3,4,5]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Limited Backward Search in Nearly Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Find correct position for an element by searching only k positions backward instead of all previous positions.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that in a k-sorted array, each element is at most k positions away from its sorted position. This means when inserting an element at index i, we only need to search backwards up to k positions (from i-1 to i-k) to find where it belongs. The algorithm starts with the element to insert (key) and compares it with elements from right to left within this limited window. If an element is greater than key, we shift it right to make space. We stop when we find an element ≤ key or when we've searched k positions. Then we insert key at the next position. Time complexity is O(k) for the search. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented within the insertion subroutine. Common mistakes include searching beyond k positions (wasting time) or not searching enough positions (causing incorrect sorting). The key intuition is the k-sorted property guarantees correctness: if key were more than k positions left, the array wouldn't be k-sorted initially. This optimization transforms insertion sort from O(n²) to O(nk), which is efficient when k is small.

examples.trace:
Find position for arr[3]=4 with k=2
Start at j = i-1 = 2
Window: indices 2 and 1 (since i-k = 1)
Compare with arr[2]=5: 5 > 4 → shift needed, continue
Compare with arr[1]=3: 3 < 4 → stop
Insert position = j+1 = 1+1 = 2
So 4 belongs at index 2

Find position for arr[3]=25 with k=1
Start at j = i-1 = 2
Window: only index 2 (since i-k = 2)
Compare with arr[2]=30: 30 > 25 → shift needed, continue
j becomes 1, but j < 2 (window end), stop
Insert position = j+1 = 1+1 = 2
So 25 belongs at index 2

ladders.title: Building Block — Shift Array Elements Right to Make Space
ladders.desc: Move elements in an array to the right to create space for insertion at a specific position.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for insertion-based sorting algorithms. The intuition is that when we need to insert an element into a sorted or nearly sorted portion of an array, we may need to shift existing elements to the right to create space at the correct insertion point. The algorithm works by starting from the end of the segment to be shifted and moving each element one position to the right. We typically do this in reverse order (from right to left) to avoid overwriting elements. For example, to shift elements from index start to end-1 one position right, we set arr[end] = arr[end-1], then arr[end-1] = arr[end-2], and so on until arr[start+1] = arr[start]. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the number of elements shifted. Space complexity is O(1) as it's done in-place. Connection to L2: This shifting operation is performed during the backward search when we find elements greater than the key. Common mistakes include shifting in the wrong direction (left instead of right), shifting too many or too few elements, or overwriting data by shifting in forward order. Proper shifting preserves all existing elements while creating exactly one empty slot for the new element.

examples.trace:
Shift elements from index 1 to 3 right by one position
Start: [1,2,3,4,_,5] (underscore represents empty slot)
We want to shift indices 1,2,3 to 2,3,4
Do in reverse order:
First shift index 3 to 4: arr[4] = arr[3] → [1,2,3,4,4,5]
Then shift index 2 to 3: arr[3] = arr[2] → [1,2,3,3,4,5]
Then shift index 1 to 2: arr[2] = arr[1] → [1,2,2,3,4,5]
Now index 1 is empty for insertion
Result after shifts: [1,2,2,3,4,5]

Shift elements from index 0 to 1 right by one position
Start: [5,6,7,_,8]
Shift index 1 to 2: arr[2] = arr[1] → [5,6,6,_,8]
Shift index 0 to 1: arr[1] = arr[0] → [5,5,6,_,8]
Now index 0 is empty for insertion
Result after shifts: [5,5,6,7,8] (note: original 7 at index 2 was overwritten, but in full algorithm we'd handle this differently)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Swap/Shift Decision
ladders.desc: Compare two elements and decide whether to shift one right based on the comparison result.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is the atomic action in insertion sort. The intuition is simple: when inserting a new element into a sorted sequence, we compare it with existing elements to determine its correct position. The algorithm involves comparing the element to insert (key) with an existing element arr[j]. If arr[j] > key, then key belongs before arr[j], so we need to shift arr[j] right to make space. If arr[j] <= key, then key belongs after arr[j], so we stop comparing and insert key at the next position. This decision is made repeatedly during the insertion process. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison triggers the shift operation when arr[j] > key. Common mistakes include using the wrong comparison operator (e.g., < instead of >), or not handling equal elements correctly (should stop when arr[j] <= key, not just <). This operation embodies the core sorting logic: we maintain that elements to the left of the current position are sorted, and each new element is placed in correct order relative to them.

examples.trace:
Compare key=5 with arr[j]=8
Is 8 > 5? Yes
Decision: shift arr[j] right because key is smaller

Compare key=7 with arr[j]=4
Is 4 > 7? No
Decision: stop shifting and insert key after arr[j] because key is larger or equal

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Array Indexing and Element Comparison
ladders.desc: Access array elements by index and compare numerical values to determine order.
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental prerequisite for any sorting algorithm. Arrays are data structures that store elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible via indices starting from 0. To work with arrays, you need to understand how to read arr[i] to get the element at position i, and how to write arr[i] = value to modify an element. Comparison of numerical values is basic: for two numbers a and b, we can check if a > b, a < b, or a == b. In sorting, we use these comparisons to determine the relative order of elements. For example, if we want ascending order, we arrange elements so that each element is less than or equal to the next one. Time complexity for accessing or comparing is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L4: This foundation enables the compare-and-shift decision. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices (e.g., accessing arr[n] when valid indices are 0 to n-1), confusing comparison operators, or not understanding zero-based indexing. Beginners should practice simple array operations and comparisons before attempting sorting algorithms.

examples.input: arr = [5, 2, 8, 1], access arr[2], compare with arr[0]
examples.trace:
Array indices: 0:5, 1:2, 2:8, 3:1
Access arr[2]: value at index 2 is 8
Access arr[0]: value at index 0 is 5
Compare 8 and 5: 8 > 5 is true

examples.input: arr = [3, 7, 4, 9], access arr[1], compare with arr[3]
examples.trace:
Array indices: 0:3, 1:7, 2:4, 3:9
Access arr[1]: value at index 1 is 7
Access arr[3]: value at index 3 is 9
Compare 7 and 9: 7 < 9 is true

testCases.input: arr = [10, 20, 30], access arr[0], compare with arr[1]
arr = [5, 3, 8], access arr[2], compare with arr[0]
arr = [1, 1, 1], access arr[1], compare with arr[2]
arr = [9, 4, 6], access arr[1], compare with arr[0]
arr = [2, 5, 7, 3], access arr[3], compare with arr[1]
arr = [100, 50, 75], access arr[0], compare with arr[2]
arr = [6, 2, 9, 4], access arr[2], compare with arr[3]
arr = [8, 8, 8], access arr[0], compare with arr[1]
arr = [3, 6, 1], access arr[1], compare with arr[0]
arr = [12, 15, 10], access arr[2], compare with arr[0]

approaches.name: Min-Heap O(n log k)
approaches.summary: Use a min-heap of size k+1 to maintain the smallest elements within a sliding window, extracting the minimum and adding the next element iteratively. This optimally exploits the k-sorted property for efficient sorting.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort K-Sorted Array Using Min-Heap
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to sort a nearly sorted array where each element is at most k positions away from its sorted position.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach leverages the k-sorted property: in a k-sorted array, the smallest element in the entire array must be within the first k+1 elements. By maintaining a min-heap of size k+1, we can efficiently extract the minimum element at each step while sliding through the array. This works because after extracting the minimum, the next smallest element must be among the remaining elements in the heap or the next incoming element. The algorithm initializes a min-heap with the first k+1 elements (or all elements if n ≤ k+1). Then, for each position in the sorted result, it extracts the minimum from the heap and adds the next element from the array (if any remain). This ensures we always have the smallest available element in the heap. Time complexity is O(n log k) since we perform n insertions and extractions on a heap of size at most k+1. Space complexity is O(k) for the heap. Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the key subroutine of heap management with the sliding window traversal. Common mistakes include incorrect heap size (should be k+1, not k), forgetting to handle cases where n ≤ k+1, and off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: def sort_k_sorted(arr, k): n = len(arr); heap = arr[:min(k+1, n)]; heapq.heapify(heap); result = []; for i in range(min(k+1, n), n): result.append(heapq.heappop(heap)); heapq.heappush(heap, arr[i]); while heap: result.append(heapq.heappop(heap)); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize heap with first k+1 = 4 elements: [6,5,3,2] → heapify to [2,5,3,6]. Step 2: i=4 (arr[4]=8): pop min=2 → result=[2]; push 8 → heap=[3,5,8,6]. Step 3: i=5 (arr[5]=10): pop min=3 → result=[2,3]; push 10 → heap=[5,6,8,10]. Step 4: i=6 (arr[6]=9): pop min=5 → result=[2,3,5]; push 9 → heap=[6,9,8,10]. Step 5: i=7 (end): pop min=6 → result=[2,3,5,6]; heap=[8,9,10]. Step 6: pop min=8 → result=[2,3,5,6,8]; heap=[9,10]. Step 7: pop min=9 → result=[2,3,5,6,8,9]; heap=[10]. Step 8: pop min=10 → result=[2,3,5,6,8,9,10]; heap empty.

Step 1: Heap with first 5 elements: [10,9,8,7,4] → heapify to [4,7,8,10,9]. Step 2: i=5 (arr[5]=70): pop min=4 → result=[4]; push 70 → heap=[7,9,8,10,70]. Step 3: i=6 (arr[6]=60): pop min=7 → result=[4,7]; push 60 → heap=[8,9,70,10,60]. Step 4: i=7 (arr[7]=50): pop min=8 → result=[4,7,8]; push 50 → heap=[9,10,70,60,50]. Step 5: i=8 (end): pop min=9 → result=[4,7,8,9]; heap=[10,50,70,60]. Step 6: pop min=10 → result=[4,7,8,9,10]; heap=[50,60,70]. Step 7: pop min=50 → result=[4,7,8,9,10,50]; heap=[60,70]. Step 8: pop min=60 → result=[4,7,8,9,10,50,60]; heap=[70]. Step 9: pop min=70 → result=[4,7,8,9,10,50,60,70]; heap empty.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sliding Window Heap Management
ladders.desc: Maintain a min-heap of size k+1 while traversing an array, extracting min and adding next element.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core engine of the k-sorted sorting algorithm. The intuition is that by keeping a sliding window of k+1 elements in a min-heap, we can always access the smallest element in that window efficiently. This works because in a k-sorted array, the global minimum must lie within the first k+1 elements, and after removing it, the next minimum must be within the updated window. The algorithm: initialize heap with first min(k+1, n) elements and heapify. Then, for index i from k+1 to n-1: extract min from heap (this becomes the next sorted element), add arr[i] to heap. Finally, extract remaining elements from heap. Time: O(n log k) for n heap operations on size ≤ k+1. Space: O(k) for heap. Connection to L0: This subroutine directly implements the main loop of the full solution. Common mistakes: not handling when n ≤ k+1 (should heapify all elements), incorrect extraction order, or forgetting to push after popping. Real code pattern: def sliding_heap(arr, k): heap = arr[:min(k+1, len(arr))]; heapq.heapify(heap); result = []; for i in range(min(k+1, len(arr)), len(arr)): result.append(heapq.heappop(heap)); heapq.heappush(heap, arr[i]); while heap: result.append(heapq.heappop(heap)); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Heap with first 3 elements: [3,2,1] → heapify to [1,2,3]. Step 2: i=3 (arr[3]=5): pop min=1 → result=[1]; push 5 → heap=[2,3,5]. Step 3: i=4 (arr[4]=4): pop min=2 → result=[1,2]; push 4 → heap=[3,5,4]. Step 4: i=5 (end): pop min=3 → result=[1,2,3]; heap=[4,5]. Step 5: pop min=4 → result=[1,2,3,4]; heap=[5]. Step 6: pop min=5 → result=[1,2,3,4,5]; heap empty.

Array [2,1,3] is 1-sorted (each element ≤1 position away from sorted order). Heap with first 2 elements: [2,1] → heapify to [1,2]. Step 1: i=2 (arr[2]=3): pop min=1 → result=[1]; push 3 → heap=[2,3]. Step 2: i=3 (end): pop min=2 → result=[1,2]; heap=[3]. Step 3: pop min=3 → result=[1,2,3]; heap empty. Final sorted output: [1,2,3].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Exploiting K-Sorted Property with Heap
ladders.desc: Understand why a min-heap of size k+1 can efficiently sort a nearly sorted array.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that in a k-sorted array, each element is at most k positions away from its final sorted position. This means the smallest element in the entire array must be within the first k+1 elements (index 0 to k). By maintaining a min-heap of these k+1 elements, we can extract the minimum in O(log k) time. After removing it, the next smallest element must be among the remaining k elements in the heap or the next element we add (since all elements are within k positions). This creates a sliding window: as we move through the array, we add the next element to the heap and extract the new minimum. The intuition is similar to a priority queue that always contains the candidates for the next smallest element. This reduces the problem from O(n log n) general sorting to O(n log k). Time analysis: building initial heap O(k), then n-k-1 insertions and extractions each O(log k), total O(n log k). Space: O(k) for heap. Connection to L1: This logic justifies the sliding window heap management. Common mistakes: misunderstanding k (distance in sorted order, not index difference), using heap size k instead of k+1, or not realizing the property holds for all elements. Real code pattern: The key idea is that for i from 0 to n-1, the element at sorted position i must be in arr[i-k : i+k+1] (with bounds), so a heap covering i to i+k works.

examples.trace: Array is 1-sorted. Sorted order: [1,2,3,4,5]. Check property: Element 1 is at index 1 in original, distance 1 from sorted index 0. Element 2 at index 0, distance 1 from sorted index 1. Element 3 at index 2, distance 0. Element 4 at index 4, distance 1 from sorted index 3. Element 5 at index 3, distance 1 from sorted index 4. All within k=1. Heap of size k+1=2: start with [2,1] → min=1 (correct first sorted). After removing 1, heap has [2], add next element 3 → heap [2,3] → min=2 (correct second). Continue.

Array is 3-sorted. Sorted: [2,3,5,6,8,9,10]. Check: 2 at index 3, distance 3 to sorted index 0. 3 at index 2, distance 2 to sorted index 1. 5 at index 1, distance 1 to sorted index 2. 6 at index 0, distance 3 to sorted index 3. 8 at index 4, distance 1 to sorted index 4. 10 at index 5, distance 1 to sorted index 5. 9 at index 6, distance 1 to sorted index 6. All within k=3. Heap size 4: first 4 elements [6,5,3,2] → min=2 (correct first). After removing, add 8 → heap [3,5,6,8] → min=3 (correct second), etc.

testCases.expected: All elements at correct positions, distance 0
Element 1 at index 1, distance 1 to sorted index 0
All elements within 2 positions of sorted order
Element 2 at index 3, distance 3 to sorted index 0
Element 1 at index 1, distance 1 to sorted index 0
Element 1 at index 3, distance 3 to sorted index 0, within k=4
Each element within 2 positions of sorted order

ladders.title: Building Block — Min-Heap Operations and Properties
ladders.desc: Implement and understand min-heap: insert, extract-min, heapify.
ladders.explanation: A min-heap is a complete binary tree where each node is smaller than or equal to its children, ensuring the minimum element is at the root. This structure supports efficient insertions and extractions in O(log n) time. The intuition is that by maintaining the heap property after each operation, we can quickly access the smallest element. Key operations: insert(val): add to end, then bubble up (compare with parent, swap if smaller). extract_min(): remove root, replace with last element, then bubble down (compare with children, swap with smaller child). heapify(arr): build heap from array in O(n) time by bubbling down from last non-leaf node. Time: insert and extract-min are O(log n) where n is heap size; heapify is O(n). Space: O(n) to store heap. Connection to L2: Min-heap enables the efficient sliding window in k-sorted sorting. Common mistakes: incorrect bubble up/down logic, not handling empty heap, or off-by-one in array indices (heap often stored in array with root at index 0). Real code pattern: class MinHeap: def __init__(self): self.heap = []; def insert(self, val): self.heap.append(val); self._bubble_up(len(self.heap)-1); def extract_min(self): if not self.heap: return None; min_val = self.heap[0]; self.heap[0] = self.heap[-1]; self.heap.pop(); if self.heap: self._bubble_down(0); return min_val; def _bubble_up(self, i): while i>0 and self.heap[i] < self.heap[(i-1)//2]: swap; i = (i-1)//2; def _bubble_down(self, i): n = len(self.heap); while True: left = 2*i+1; right = 2*i+2; smallest = i; if left < n and self.heap[left] < self.heap[smallest]: smallest = left; if right < n and self.heap[right] < self.heap[smallest]: smallest = right; if smallest != i: swap; i = smallest; else: break.

examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 5 → heap [5]. Step 2: Insert 3 → heap [5,3]; bubble up: 3 < 5? yes, swap → [3,5]. Step 3: Insert 8 → heap [3,5,8]; bubble up: 8 < 5? no, stop. Step 4: Insert 1 → heap [3,5,8,1]; bubble up: 1 < 3? yes, swap → [1,5,8,3]; bubble up: 1 < parent? root, stop. Final heap: [1,5,8,3] but actually after bubble down during extract or proper heapify, it should be [1,3,8,5]. Let's correct: After insert 1: heap [3,5,8,1]; compare 1 with parent 5 at index 1: 1 < 5, swap → [3,1,8,5]; compare 1 with parent 3 at index 0: 1 < 3, swap → [1,3,8,5]. So final is [1,3,8,5].

Step 1: Extract min: root=1. Step 2: Replace root with last element 5 → heap [5,3,8]. Step 3: Bubble down: compare 5 with children 3 and 8; 3 is smaller, swap 5 and 3 → heap [3,5,8]. Step 4: Compare 5 with children (none or out of bounds), stop. Final heap: [3,5,8].

testCases.input: Insert 10, 20, 5 into empty heap, then extract min

testCases.expected: First extract returns 3, heap [5,7]; second returns 5, heap [7]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Traversal and Element Swapping
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array, access elements, and perform swaps.
ladders.explanation: This foundational operation involves looping through an array using indices, accessing elements at specific positions, and swapping values between indices. The intuition is that arrays are contiguous memory locations, and we can manipulate them by iterating from start to end or in other patterns. Key concepts: use a for loop with index i from 0 to n-1 (where n is array length) to traverse; access element with arr[i]; swap two elements at indices i and j using a temporary variable: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp. Time: O(n) for traversal, O(1) for access and swap. Space: O(1) extra for temp variable. Connection to L3: Array traversal is used in heap operations to manage the heap array. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in loop bounds, forgetting to use temp in swap (direct assignment loses data), or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: def traverse_and_swap(arr): n = len(arr); for i in range(n): print(arr[i]); # Example swap first and last if n>1: temp = arr[0]; arr[0] = arr[n-1]; arr[n-1] = temp; return arr.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access arr[1]=2, arr[3]=4. Step 2: temp = arr[1] = 2. Step 3: arr[1] = arr[3] = 4. Step 4: arr[3] = temp = 2. Result: [1,4,3,2,5].

examples.input: Traverse arr = [10, 20, 30] and print each element
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=10, print 10. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=20, print 20. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=30, print 30.

testCases.input: arr = [1, 2, 3, 4], traverse and return last element

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand what an array is, how to create one, and access elements by index.
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index starting from 0. The intuition is that it's like a row of boxes numbered 0, 1, 2, etc., where you can quickly look up or change the value in any box by its number. Key concepts: declaration (e.g., arr = [1, 2, 3] in Python), length (len(arr)), accessing element at index i (arr[i]), and modifying (arr[i] = new_value). Time: O(1) for access and modification. Space: O(n) to store n elements. Connection to L4: Arrays are the basic container used in traversal and swapping. Common mistakes: using index out of range (e.g., arr[5] for array of size 5 is invalid), confusing 0-based indexing (first element is arr[0]), or not initializing array properly. Real code pattern: arr = [10, 20, 30]; print(arr[0]) # outputs 10; arr[1] = 25; # now arr is [10, 25, 30]; print(len(arr)) # outputs 3.

examples.input: Create array with elements 5, 10, 15, access index 2
examples.trace: Step 1: arr = [5, 10, 15]. Step 2: arr[2] refers to third element (since indices: 0→5, 1→10, 2→15). Step 3: Value is 15.
Step 1: arr = [1, 2, 3, 4]. Step 2: arr[1] = 9. Step 3: Now arr is [1, 9, 3, 4].
